summary: Spolsky introduces the concept of Activity Based Planning as a superior approach to UI design. He argues that many designers start by listing features and attaching them to menu items, which results in software that has the right functionality but poor usability. Instead, he advocates for Microsoft's approach of identifying the activities users are actually trying to accomplish and designing around those workflows. The post serves as a teaser for the final chapter of his book on UI design.
key_points: Design principles help evaluate existing designs but don't tell you where to start
Feature-driven design (listing features and hanging them off menus) leads to software that doesn't flow right
Activity Based Planning focuses on what the user is trying to accomplish rather than what features to expose
The concept originated from Mike Conte on Microsoft's Excel team
money_quotes: Many people write big, functional outlines of all the features they thought up. Then they design each one, and hang it off of a menu item. When they're done, the program has all the functionality they wanted, but it doesn't flow right.
People sit down and they don't know what it does, and they don't know how to accomplish what they want.
The key insight is to figure out the activity that the user is doing, and focus on making it easy to accomplish that activity.
key_insight: Good UI design starts not by listing features but by identifying the activities users want to accomplish and designing the experience around those workflows.
